content:
# James Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 262 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_001_page_001.jpg

---

**1:1** Iakobos, of-God and of-[the]-Lord Iesous Christou a-slave, to-the twelve tribes, the [ones] in the dispersion: To-rejoice.

**1:2** All joy consider [it], my brothers, whenever you-fall-into trials various,

**1:3** knowing that the testing of-your faith works-out endurance.

**1:4** And let-the endurance have [its] work complete, in-order-that you-may-be complete and whole, in nothing lacking.

**1:5** But if any of-you lacks wisdom, let-him-ask from the [one] giving — God — to-all generously and not reproaching, and it-will-be-given to-him.

**1:6** But let-him-ask in faith, nothing doubting; for the [one] doubting is-like a-wave of-[the]-sea, being-driven-by-wind and being-tossed.

**1:7** For let- not -suppose that man that he-will-receive anything from the Lord,

**1:8** [he is] a-double-souled man, unstable in all his ways.

**1:9** But let-boast the brother, the lowly [one], in his exaltation,

**1:10** and the rich [one] in his humiliation, because as a-flower of-grass he-will-pass-away.

**1:11** For the sun rose with the scorching-heat and withered the grass, and the flower of-it fell-off, and the beauty of-the face of-it perished; so also the rich [one] in his goings will-be-withered.

**1:12** Blessed [is the] man who endures trial, because having-become approved he-will-receive the crown of-the life which he-promised to-the [ones] loving him.

**1:13** Let- no-one being-tempted -say that "From God I-am-being-tempted"; for God is untemptable by-evils, and he himself tempts no-one.

**1:14** But each [one] is-tempted by his-own desire, being-dragged-out and being-enticed.

**1:15** Then the desire, having-conceived, gives-birth-to sin; and the sin, having-been-completed, brings-forth death.

**1:16** Do- not -be-deceived, my beloved brothers.

**1:17** Every giving good and every gift complete is from-above, coming-down from the Father of-the lights, with whom there-is no variation or of-turning shadow.

**1:18** Having-willed, he-brought- us -forth by-a-word of-truth, for us to-be a-certain first-fruit of-his creatures.

**1:19** Know [this], my beloved brothers: but let- every man -be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath;

**1:20** for [the] wrath of-a-man [the] righteousness of-God does- not -work.

**1:21** Therefore, having-put-away all filthiness and abundance of-evil, in gentleness receive the implanted word, the [one] being-able to-save your souls.

**1:22** But become doers of-[the]-word and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves.

**1:23** Because if anyone is a-hearer of-[the]-word and not a-doer, this-one is-like a-man observing the face of-his origin in a-mirror;

**1:24** for he-observed himself and has-gone-away and immediately forgot what-sort [of person] he-was.

**1:25** But the [one] having-looked-into [the] perfect law, the [law] of-the freedom, and having-remained-beside [it], not having-become a-hearer of-forgetfulness but a-doer of-work — this-one will-be blessed in his doing.

**1:26** If anyone considers [himself] to-be religious, not bridling his tongue but deceiving his heart, the religion of-this-one [is] worthless.

**1:27** Religion pure and undefiled before the God and Father is this: to-visit orphans and widows in their affliction, [and] to-keep oneself unstained from the world.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **1:1 — "Iakobos"**: Sinaiticus reads ΙΑΚΩΒΟC (Iakobos). The author identifies himself as a slave (doulos) of both God and the Lord Jesus Christ. The standard form is preserved; no variant of note.

2. **1:3 — "testing" (dokimion)**: Sinaiticus reads ΔΟΚΙΜΙΟΝ. Some witnesses read ΔΟΚΙΜΕΙΟΝ (variant spelling). The sense is "the proving/testing instrument" of faith — not the result of testing but the means of it.

3. **1:11 — Aorist sequence**: The verbs ΑΝΕΤΕΙΛΕΝ (rose), ΕΞΗΡΑΝΕΝ (withered), ΕΞΕΠΕCΕΝ (fell off), ΑΠΩΛΕΤΟ (perished) are all aorist — gnomic aorists describing a timeless process. English past tense is used to preserve the Greek tense-form literally.

4. **1:12 — "he promised" (singular)**: Sinaiticus reads ΕΠΗΓΓΕΙΛΑΤΟ without explicit subject. The implied subject is debated: God or the Lord. The singular "he" is retained without specification, as the manuscript does not clarify. Some later manuscripts read "the Lord" (ο κυριος) as a gloss.

5. **1:17 — "variation or of-turning shadow"**: Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΑΛΛΑΓΗ Η ΤΡΟΠΗC ΑΠΟCΚΙΑCΜΑ. The phrase is notoriously difficult. Literally: "variation or of-turning a-shadow." The genitive ΤΡΟΠΗC ("of turning") likely modifies ΑΠΟCΚΙΑCΜΑ ("shadow"), yielding "shadow cast by turning." The astronomical metaphor refers to celestial bodies casting shifting shadows; God does not shift.

6. **1:17 — "giving good" vs. "good giving"**: Sinaiticus reads ΔΟCΙC ΑΓΑΘΗ — "giving good" (noun + adjective). Some witnesses reverse the word order. The Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

7. **1:19 — "Know" (ΙCΤΕ)**: Sinaiticus appears to read ΙCΤΕ, which could be either imperative ("Know!") or indicative ("You know"). Context favors the imperative. Some manuscripts read ΩCΤΕ ("therefore"), which is a significant variant affecting the logical flow. Sinaiticus supports ΙCΤΕ.

8. **1:20 — Word order**: Literally "for wrath of-man righteousness of-God does-not work." The word order is preserved to show the emphatic fronting of "wrath of a man" in the Greek.

9. **1:22 — "deceiving yourselves" (paralogizomenoi)**: The participle ΠΑΡΑΛΟΓΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΙ means "reasoning falsely alongside" — self-deception through faulty logic, not mere ignorance.

10. **1:25 — "law of the freedom"**: Sinaiticus reads ΝΟΜΟΝ ΤΕΛΕΙΟΝ ΤΟΝ ΤΗC ΕΛΕΥΘΕΡΙΑC — "perfect law, the [one] of the freedom." The article with "freedom" (ΤΗC ΕΛΕΥΘΕΡΙΑC) makes it a specific, known freedom, not freedom in general.

11. **1:26 — ΘΡΗCΚΟC / ΘΡΗCΚΕΙΑ**: The cognate pair "religious" (threskos) and "religion" (threskeia) appears only here and in 1:27 in the entire New Testament. The terms denote outward, ritualistic religious observance — cultus rather than inner piety.

12. **General — Scribal features**: Sinaiticus exhibits its characteristic features in this column: nomina sacra (ΘΥ for ΘΕΟΥ, ΚΥ for ΚΥΡΙΟΥ, ΙΥ ΧΥ for ΙΗCΟΥ ΧΡΙCΤΟΥ), no word spacing (scriptio continua), and occasional corrections visible in the text. The scribe of James is identified as Scribe A.
